questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Ivan Pavlov's original research with dogs demonstrated which type of learning?

choices.explanation: Operant conditioning involves learning through consequences of behavior (Skinner), not through stimulus pairing as in Pavlov's work.
Observational learning involves watching others, not pairing neutral stimuli with unconditioned stimuli as Pavlov demonstrated.
Pavlov paired a neutral stimulus (bell) with food (unconditioned stimulus) until the bell alone elicited salivation — the hallmark of classical conditioning.
Insight learning (Köhler) involves sudden problem-solving, not stimulus-response pairing.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: In classical conditioning, what is the unconditioned stimulus (US)?

choices.text: A stimulus that naturally triggers a response without prior learning
choices.explanation: The unconditioned stimulus automatically and naturally produces an unconditioned response without prior learning (e.g., food produces salivation).

choices.text: A previously neutral stimulus that has been paired with the US
choices.explanation: This describes the conditioned stimulus (CS) — a formerly neutral stimulus that triggers a response after pairing with the US.

choices.text: The learned response triggered by the conditioned stimulus
choices.explanation: This describes the conditioned response (CR) — the learned response to the CS after conditioning.

choices.text: A stimulus that has been extinguished through non-reinforcement
choices.explanation: Extinction involves weakening a conditioned response, not defining the unconditioned stimulus.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: After extinction, a conditioned response that reappears after a rest period is called:

choices.explanation: Generalization is responding to stimuli similar to the conditioned stimulus, not the reappearance after rest.
Discrimination is responding only to the specific conditioned stimulus, not to similar ones — not related to rest period reappearance.
Acquisition is the initial learning phase of classical conditioning, not the reappearance of an extinguished response.
Spontaneous recovery is the reappearance of a previously extinguished conditioned response after a rest period without additional CS-US pairings.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A young child burned their hand on a hot stove. Now whenever the child sees any stove — electric, gas, or camping — they cry and pull away.

questions.question: This child's response to all types of stoves best illustrates which classical conditioning concept?

choices.explanation: Extinction involves the weakening of a conditioned response when the CS is presented without the US — the opposite of what is described here.
Stimulus generalization occurs when stimuli similar to the original CS (hot stove) also elicit the conditioned response — the child responds to all stoves, not just the specific one.
Discrimination would mean responding only to the specific stove that caused pain, not generalizing to all stoves.
Spontaneous recovery describes the reappearance of an extinguished response after rest, not responding to similar stimuli.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: B.F. Skinner's research focused primarily on which type of learning?

choices.explanation: Classical conditioning was Pavlov's focus — Skinner studied how consequences of behavior shape future behavior.
Skinner developed operant conditioning, demonstrating that behavior is shaped by reinforcements and punishments using tools like the Skinner box.
Observational learning was studied by Bandura, not Skinner.
Latent learning was demonstrated by Tolman, not Skinner.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A teacher gives a student a gold star every time they correctly answer a question. This is an example of:

choices.explanation: Negative reinforcement involves removing an aversive stimulus to increase behavior — adding a gold star is the opposite.
Positive punishment adds an unpleasant stimulus to decrease behavior — a gold star reward does not decrease behavior.
Positive reinforcement adds a desirable stimulus (gold star) following a behavior to increase the likelihood of that behavior occurring again.
Negative punishment removes a desirable stimulus to decrease behavior — adding a gold star does not remove anything.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Taking away a teenager's phone because they came home late is an example of which operant conditioning principle?

choices.explanation: Positive reinforcement adds a pleasant stimulus to increase behavior — taking away a phone is removal of something, not addition.
Negative reinforcement removes an aversive stimulus to increase a desired behavior — removing a phone is removing something desirable, not aversive.
Positive punishment adds an aversive stimulus to decrease behavior — this example removes something, not adds.
Negative punishment removes a desirable stimulus (phone) following an undesired behavior (coming home late) to decrease that behavior.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which reinforcement schedule produces the highest and most consistent response rate?

choices.explanation: Fixed-ratio schedules produce high response rates, but there is a brief pause after each reinforcement — not as consistent as variable-ratio.
Fixed-interval schedules produce a scalloped pattern with low responding right after reinforcement — not the highest or most consistent rate.
Variable-interval schedules produce slow, steady responding but not the highest response rates.
Variable-ratio schedules reinforce after an unpredictable number of responses, producing the highest and most consistent response rates with the greatest resistance to extinction.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A casino slot machine rewards players on a variable-ratio schedule. This explains why gambling behavior is:

choices.text: Easy to extinguish because reinforcement is infrequent
choices.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules actually produce the greatest resistance to extinction, making gambling behaviors very difficult to extinguish.

choices.text: Difficult to extinguish and maintained at a high response rate
choices.explanation: Variable-ratio reinforcement creates unpredictable reinforcement, which produces both the highest response rates and the greatest resistance to extinction — making gambling very persistent.

choices.text: Easy to extinguish because reinforcement is predictable
choices.explanation: Reinforcement in variable-ratio schedules is specifically unpredictable, which makes the behavior harder, not easier, to extinguish.

choices.text: Reinforced only during specific time intervals
choices.explanation: Time-based reinforcement describes interval schedules, not the ratio schedule used by slot machines.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The process of reinforcing successive approximations of a desired behavior is called:

choices.explanation: Extinction refers to the weakening of a response through removal of reinforcement, not the step-by-step building of a new behavior.
Generalization is responding to stimuli similar to the conditioned stimulus, not the incremental building of a new behavior.
Shaping uses differential reinforcement of successive approximations — progressively reinforcing behaviors that come closer and closer to the desired target behavior.
Modeling involves learning by observing others, not reinforcing step-by-step approximations.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Edward Tolman's maze experiments with rats demonstrated that learning can occur without reinforcement. This type of learning is called:

choices.explanation: Insight learning is sudden problem solving through understanding, studied by Köhler, not Tolman.
Tolman showed that rats formed cognitive maps of mazes even without reinforcement — this hidden, unreinforced learning that only shows when needed is latent learning.
Vicarious learning occurs by observing others being reinforced or punished — it requires watching someone else, not independent exploration.
Instinctive drift is when conditioned behaviors revert toward instinctive behaviors — unrelated to Tolman's cognitive map experiments.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Albert Bandura's Bobo doll experiments demonstrated which type of learning?

choices.explanation: Classical conditioning involves pairing stimuli — Bandura's study had children watching adults behave aggressively, not stimulus pairing.
Operant conditioning involves reinforcement and punishment — Bandura showed children learned by watching, not by receiving consequences themselves.
Children who observed adults behaving aggressively toward a Bobo doll later imitated that aggression — demonstrating observational (social) learning without direct reinforcement.
Latent learning is learning that is unexpressed until needed (Tolman) — Bandura's children immediately expressed the observed behavior.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Observational learning requires which four processes, according to Bandura?

choices.explanation: Bandura's four components of observational learning are: attention (noticing the behavior), retention (remembering it), reproduction (being able to perform it), and motivation (having a reason to perform it).
These are the four key processes in classical conditioning, not in Bandura's model of observational learning.
These are operant conditioning concepts, not the components of observational learning.
These describe the memory process, not specifically the components of observational learning.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Wolfgang Köhler's research with chimpanzees contributed to our understanding of learning by demonstrating:

choices.text: That animals can learn through classical conditioning
choices.explanation: Classical conditioning was already established by Pavlov — Köhler's chimps demonstrated something qualitatively different.

choices.text: That learning requires multiple reinforced trials
choices.explanation: Köhler's chimps showed learning that did NOT require multiple reinforced trials — it happened suddenly.

choices.text: Insight learning — sudden problem solving through mental reorganization
choices.explanation: Köhler showed that chimps could suddenly solve problems (like stacking boxes to reach bananas) without trial-and-error — demonstrating insight learning through cognitive reorganization.

choices.text: That observational learning is the primary means of animal learning
choices.explanation: Köhler's chimps worked alone on problems — the insight came from individual cognitive reorganization, not watching others.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: John Garcia's research on taste aversion showed that classical conditioning:

choices.text: Requires the NS and US to be presented together many times before conditioning occurs
choices.explanation: Garcia showed the opposite — taste aversion can form in a single trial, violating the assumption that multiple pairings are needed.

choices.text: Is limited to responses that are naturally reinforced by immediate rewards
choices.explanation: Taste aversion demonstrates classical conditioning can occur with a long delay between CS and US — violating the contiguity assumption.

choices.text: Can be constrained by biological predispositions — some associations are learned more easily
choices.explanation: Garcia showed that rats easily associate taste with illness (biological preparedness) but not taste with shock, demonstrating that biological constraints shape what can be classically conditioned.

choices.text: Requires conscious awareness of the CS-US relationship
choices.explanation: Taste aversion forms unconsciously — the rat doesn't need to understand the relationship between the taste and the illness.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of negative reinforcement?

choices.text: A child is spanked for misbehaving
choices.explanation: This is positive punishment — adding an aversive stimulus (spanking) to decrease behavior.

choices.text: A student loses TV privileges for failing a test
choices.explanation: This is negative punishment — removing a pleasant stimulus (TV) to decrease undesired behavior.

choices.text: Taking aspirin to relieve a headache, which reinforces pill-taking behavior
choices.explanation: Negative reinforcement removes an aversive stimulus (headache pain) following a behavior (taking aspirin), increasing the likelihood of taking aspirin again. The 'negative' refers to removal, not unpleasantness.

choices.text: Receiving a bonus for high sales performance
choices.explanation: This is positive reinforcement — adding a pleasant stimulus (bonus) to increase a desired behavior (high sales).

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A stimulus that was once neutral but gained reinforcing power through association with a primary reinforcer is called a:

choices.explanation: An unconditioned stimulus naturally elicits a response without prior learning — it is not learned through association.

A conditioned (secondary) reinforcer gains its reinforcing value through association with a primary reinforcer — money is the classic example, as it has no intrinsic value but is paired with primary reinforcers.
A discriminative stimulus signals that a particular behavior will be reinforced, but has not itself acquired reinforcing value.
Primary reinforcers (food, water) satisfy biological needs without prior learning — their reinforcing power is innate.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The tendency for conditioned responses to drift back toward instinctive behaviors is called:

choices.explanation: Spontaneous recovery is the return of an extinguished conditioned response after a rest period — not the drift back toward instinctive behavior.
Biological preparedness means organisms are predisposed to learn certain associations (e.g., taste-illness) — it does not describe behavior reverting during training.
Instinctive drift (Breland and Breland) is the tendency for learned behaviors to revert toward species-typical instinctive behaviors — biological constraints can interfere with operant conditioning.
Stimulus generalization is responding to stimuli similar to the CS — unrelated to the reversion to instinctive behaviors.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Observational learning is most likely to occur when the model is:

choices.explanation: Observers are less likely to imitate models who are unsuccessful — high-status, rewarded models are more imitated.
Age alone is not the primary factor — similarity, status, and outcomes for the model matter more in observational learning.

choices.text: Similar to the observer, high status, and seen being reinforced
choices.explanation: Bandura identified that observational learning is most likely when models are similar to observers, high in status, and seen receiving positive consequences for their behavior (vicarious reinforcement).

choices.text: A stranger the observer has never seen before
choices.explanation: Familiarity and similarity increase identification with the model, making learning more likely, not less.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher wants to train a rat to press a lever. The rat first receives food whenever it enters the correct side of the box, then only when it approaches the lever, and finally only when it actually presses it.

questions.question: The training technique described is best explained by which concept?

choices.explanation: Classical conditioning pairs stimuli to elicit reflex responses — this training technique involves reinforcing behaviors through progressive steps, which is operant conditioning.
Latent learning occurs without reinforcement — this example specifically uses food reinforcement throughout.

Shaping involves reinforcing behaviors that progressively come closer to the target behavior — reinforcing entering the correct side, then approaching, then pressing represents classic successive approximations.
Fixed-ratio refers to reinforcing after a set number of responses — the progressive changing of the criterion for reinforcement describes shaping, not a ratio schedule.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which psychologist is most closely associated with the development of operant conditioning?

choices.explanation: Pavlov developed classical conditioning through his research on salivation in dogs.
Skinner developed operant conditioning, using the Skinner box to demonstrate how consequences (reinforcements and punishments) shape behavior.
Bandura is associated with observational (social) learning and the Bobo doll studies.
Watson demonstrated classical conditioning in humans (Little Albert) and founded behaviorism, but operant conditioning is Skinner's contribution.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A child learns to fear dogs after being bitten, and later also fears cats and other four-legged animals. This is an example of:

choices.explanation: Stimulus discrimination is responding only to the specific conditioned stimulus — the child is responding to similar stimuli, which is generalization.
This fear response developed through classical conditioning (pairing the dog with pain), not through reinforcement of behavior.
After being bitten by a dog (CS-US pairing), the child generalizes the fear response to similar stimuli (cats and four-legged animals) — stimulus generalization.
Spontaneous recovery is the return of an extinguished conditioned response after a rest period — not the spread of a response to similar stimuli.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: John Watson and Rosalie Rayner's 'Little Albert' experiment demonstrated:

choices.text: That phobias can be created through classical conditioning in humans
choices.explanation: Watson conditioned a fear of white rats in 'Little Albert' by pairing the rat (CS) with a loud noise (US). This demonstrated that human emotional responses can be classically conditioned.

choices.text: That reinforcement is necessary for learning to occur in humans
choices.explanation: The Little Albert experiment used classical conditioning (stimulus pairing), not reinforcement, demonstrating fear conditioning without rewards.

choices.text: That observational learning is more powerful than direct conditioning
choices.explanation: The Little Albert study showed direct classical conditioning, not observational learning.

choices.text: That punishment is more effective than reinforcement
choices.explanation: The Little Albert study examined fear conditioning through stimulus pairing, not the effectiveness of punishment vs. reinforcement.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following best illustrates the concept of vicarious reinforcement?

choices.text: A rat presses a lever and receives food
choices.explanation: This is direct positive reinforcement — the rat itself receives food, not vicarious reinforcement.

choices.text: A child watches a sibling receive praise for cleaning their room and then cleans their own room
choices.explanation: Vicarious reinforcement occurs when observing someone else receive a reward increases the likelihood of the observer performing the same behavior — watching the sibling be praised motivates the child.

choices.text: A child is praised directly for completing homework
choices.explanation: Direct praise is positive reinforcement, not vicarious — vicarious reinforcement requires observing someone else receive reinforcement.

choices.text: A student avoids studying because a test is not for several weeks
choices.explanation: This describes procrastination or low motivation — not vicarious reinforcement through observing others.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Cognitive maps, demonstrated by Tolman, suggest that learning involves:

choices.explanation: Tolman argued against the strict behaviorist view — his rats showed learning through internal representations (cognitive maps) even without observable behavior changes until motivation arose.

choices.text: Internal mental representations that can guide behavior even without reinforcement
choices.explanation: Tolman's cognitive maps demonstrated that rats formed internal representations of their environment — evidence that learning is not purely stimulus-response, but involves cognition.

choices.text: The need for explicit reinforcement at every step
choices.explanation: Tolman's latent learning showed the opposite — rats learned maze layouts without any reinforcement, demonstrating that reinforcement is not always required for learning.
Tolman argued that rats developed learned cognitive maps, not that they had innate navigation — the cognitive map was acquired through experience.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A schedule in which a reinforcer is given after every nth response is a:

choices.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules reinforce after an unpredictable number of responses — not after a fixed number.
Fixed-interval schedules reinforce the first response after a fixed amount of time has passed — not after a specific number of responses.
Fixed-ratio schedules deliver reinforcement after every nth (fixed number of) responses — like piecework pay or earning a reward after every 10 purchases.
Variable-interval schedules reinforce the first response after varying amounts of time — not a fixed number of responses.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The overjustification effect predicts that external rewards added to an intrinsically motivating activity will:

choices.text: Permanently increase enjoyment of the activity
choices.explanation: The overjustification effect predicts the opposite — external rewards decrease intrinsic motivation over time.

choices.text: Have no effect on intrinsic motivation
choices.explanation: Research consistently shows external rewards do affect intrinsic motivation — and often reduce it.

choices.text: Reduce intrinsic motivation once the rewards are removed
choices.explanation: The overjustification effect states that adding extrinsic rewards for activities we already enjoy can undermine intrinsic motivation — once rewards are removed, people lose interest in the originally enjoyed activity.

choices.text: Have the greatest effect on interval-based behaviors
choices.explanation: The overjustification effect is about intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation — not about reinforcement schedules.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The weakening and eventual disappearance of a conditioned response when the CS is presented repeatedly without the US is called:

choices.explanation: Generalization is responding to stimuli similar to the CS, not the weakening of a response.
Discrimination is responding only to the specific CS — it does not involve weakening the response.
Extinction is the diminishing of a conditioned response when the conditioned stimulus is repeatedly presented without the unconditioned stimulus.
Acquisition is the initial phase of learning the association — the opposite of extinction.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The process of using a model's behavior to trigger an identical or similar behavior in the observer is called:

choices.explanation: Classical conditioning involves stimulus pairing to elicit automatic responses — it does not involve watching and imitating a model.
Shaping reinforces successive approximations toward a target behavior — it does not involve observation of a model.
Modeling (part of observational learning) involves imitating behaviors demonstrated by others — a key mechanism studied by Bandura.
Negative reinforcement is removal of an aversive stimulus to increase a behavior — it does not involve observing a model.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to Seligman's learned helplessness research, organisms that experience uncontrollable aversive events learn to:

choices.text: Increase their escape behaviors to regain control
choices.explanation: Seligman found the opposite — animals that experienced uncontrollable shocks became passive and stopped trying to escape even when escape was possible.

choices.text: Become passive and fail to take action even when control is possible
choices.explanation: Seligman's learned helplessness research showed that organisms exposed to uncontrollable aversive events learn that their actions are ineffective, leading to passive, helpless behavior even when the situation changes and control is possible.

choices.text: Develop stronger avoidance responses through punishment
choices.explanation: Learned helplessness produces passivity, not stronger avoidance — the organism stops responding rather than developing stronger escape behaviors.

choices.text: Generalize their control behaviors to new environments
choices.explanation: Learned helplessness shows the opposite generalization — generalization of helplessness (not control) to new situations.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: In Pavlov's experiment, what was the neutral stimulus (NS) before conditioning?

choices.explanation: Food (meat powder) was the unconditioned stimulus — it naturally triggered salivation before any conditioning.
Salivation to food was the unconditioned response — the automatic reaction to the unconditioned stimulus.
Before conditioning, the bell was a neutral stimulus that did not trigger salivation. After repeated pairing with food, it became the conditioned stimulus.

choices.text: Salivation in response to the bell
choices.explanation: Salivation in response to the bell is the conditioned response — it only occurs after conditioning, not before.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which schedule of reinforcement best describes a salesperson receiving a commission only after every 10th sale?

choices.explanation: Fixed-interval schedules reinforce the first response after a set time period, not after a specific number of responses.
Variable-ratio schedules reinforce after an unpredictable number of responses — the question specifies a fixed number (every 10th sale).
Fixed-ratio schedules deliver reinforcement after a set number of responses. Commission after every 10th sale is a fixed-ratio 10 (FR10) schedule.
Continuous reinforcement provides a reward after every single response — not after every 10th response.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Biological constraints on learning suggest that:

choices.text: All organisms can learn any association equally well given enough trials
choices.explanation: This is the 'equipotentiality' assumption that Garcia and others disproved — organisms have biological predispositions that make some associations easier to learn than others.

choices.text: Each species is biologically prepared to learn some associations more readily than others
choices.explanation: Biological preparedness means evolution has built in predispositions to learn certain associations (e.g., rats easily associate taste with illness, birds easily associate visual cues with danger) more readily than others.

choices.text: Learning is entirely determined by genetics and cannot be modified by experience
choices.explanation: Biological constraints shape but do not fully determine learning — experience still plays a crucial role.

choices.text: Classical conditioning only works with reflexive responses
choices.explanation: Classical conditioning can work with many types of responses, not just reflexes, though biological predispositions affect what is easily learned.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A rat presses a lever and immediately receives food. Later, the same rat presses the lever and no food is delivered. Over time, the rat stops pressing the lever. This illustrates:

choices.explanation: Classical conditioning involves stimulus-response pairings — pressing a lever for food is operant conditioning, so its weakening is operant extinction.
Spontaneous recovery is the reappearance of an extinguished response after rest — the question describes the extinction process itself, not recovery.
When a previously reinforced operant response (lever pressing) is no longer followed by a reinforcer (food), the response gradually decreases — this is operant extinction.
Negative punishment removes a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior — here, no food was delivered, but this describes omitting the reinforcer entirely (extinction), not actively taking away food.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Learning that occurs through watching others, without requiring direct experience, is called:

choices.explanation: Operant conditioning involves learning through direct consequences of one's own behavior — not through watching others.
Latent learning is hidden learning that occurs without direct reinforcement but through personal exploration — not necessarily by watching others.
Classical conditioning involves stimulus pairing to produce automatic responses — not learning by watching others.
Observational learning (social learning) occurs by watching and imitating others without requiring direct personal experience or reinforcement.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Higher-order conditioning occurs when:

choices.text: A previously conditioned stimulus serves as the US to condition a new neutral stimulus
choices.explanation: Higher-order (second-order) conditioning pairs a new neutral stimulus with an established CS (rather than the original US), extending the conditioning chain to new stimuli.

choices.text: An organism is reinforced for performing more complex behaviors
choices.explanation: More complex behaviors under operant conditioning describe chaining or shaping, not higher-order classical conditioning.

choices.text: A stimulus generalizes to increasingly more stimuli over time
choices.explanation: This describes stimulus generalization spreading, not higher-order conditioning.
Higher-order conditioning is within classical conditioning — it does not involve observational learning replacing anything.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following reinforcement schedules is most resistant to extinction?

choices.explanation: Continuous reinforcement produces the fastest extinction — when reinforcement suddenly stops, the organism quickly detects the change because every response was previously reinforced.
Fixed-interval schedules are less resistant to extinction than variable schedules because the pattern of reinforcement is predictable.
Fixed-ratio schedules are more resistant than continuous but less resistant to extinction than variable schedules.

Variable-ratio schedules are the most resistant to extinction because the unpredictable reinforcement makes it difficult to determine when reinforcement has permanently stopped — the organism keeps responding.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A dog that learned to salivate to a bell now also salivates to any high-pitched sound. This illustrates which classical conditioning process?

choices.explanation: Acquisition is the initial learning phase when the neutral stimulus is paired with the unconditioned stimulus — not responding to new stimuli.
Stimulus discrimination involves responding only to the specific conditioned stimulus (the bell) and NOT to similar stimuli.
Extinction is the weakening of the conditioned response — the dog here is responding MORE broadly, not less.
Stimulus generalization is the tendency to respond to stimuli similar to the original conditioned stimulus — salivating to any high-pitched sound generalizes from the original bell CS.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research on learned helplessness has been linked to which psychological disorder?

choices.explanation: Schizophrenia involves psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) — Seligman's learned helplessness research has been linked to depression, not schizophrenia.
PTSD involves trauma-related symptoms — while some overlap exists with helplessness, Seligman specifically linked learned helplessness to depression.

Seligman proposed that learned helplessness — believing one has no control over outcomes — is a cognitive model for depression. People who feel they cannot influence their situation develop depressive symptoms.
Anxiety disorders involve excessive fear and worry — while helplessness may contribute to anxiety, Seligman's model specifically focused on depression.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A child watches a parent use polite language when speaking to others and begins doing the same. This is best explained by:

choices.explanation: Positive reinforcement requires the child to receive a direct reward for using polite language — in this scenario, no direct reward is given to the child.
The child learned the behavior by watching the parent — without direct reinforcement or direct practice — which is the hallmark of observational (social) learning.
Classical conditioning involves pairing stimuli to produce reflexive responses — learning polite language by watching a parent is not stimulus-pairing.
Latent learning occurs when organisms explore an environment and form cognitive maps — this example involves imitation of a specific observed behavior, which is observational learning.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: In operant conditioning, any consequence that increases the frequency of a behavior is a:

choices.explanation: Punishment decreases the frequency of behavior — it is the opposite of reinforcement.
A reinforcer is any consequence — positive or negative — that increases the likelihood of a behavior being repeated. The key criterion is that the behavior increases.
A neutral stimulus is one that has no effect on behavior before conditioning — it does not increase behavior.
A conditioned stimulus is a term from classical conditioning (a previously neutral stimulus that now triggers a conditioned response) — not a reinforcer in the operant sense.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Classical conditioning of emotional responses, like phobias, is best treated using which behavioral therapy technique?

choices.explanation: Token economies use tokens as conditioned reinforcers to strengthen desired behaviors — they address operant conditioning, not classically conditioned fear responses.
Shaping uses successive approximations to build new behaviors through operant conditioning — it is not designed to extinguish classically conditioned fear responses.

Systematic desensitization uses classical conditioning principles — pairing the feared stimulus with relaxation responses — to extinguish phobic responses through gradual exposure and counter-conditioning.
While CBT includes both cognitive and behavioral components, the specifically behavioral technique designed to treat classically conditioned phobias is systematic desensitization.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The process of learning that an event will be followed by another event is called:

choices.explanation: Insight learning is sudden problem-solving through cognitive reorganization — not learning about event sequences.
Observational learning is learning by watching others — not learning predictive relationships between events.

Associative learning involves learning that certain events go together (CS-US in classical conditioning; behavior-consequence in operant conditioning) — both types of conditioning are forms of associative learning.
Non-associative learning (habituation, sensitization) involves changes in responding to a single repeated stimulus — not learning about relationships between two events.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A student who stops procrastinating on homework because it reduces the anxiety of an upcoming deadline is an example of:

choices.explanation: Positive reinforcement adds something pleasant to increase behavior — this example involves removing something aversive (anxiety), which is the characteristic of negative reinforcement.
Positive punishment adds something aversive to decrease a behavior — starting homework removes anxiety rather than adding an unpleasant consequence.

Doing homework removes the aversive stimulus of deadline anxiety — this removal of an unpleasant event increases the probability of doing homework early (negative reinforcement: removing something aversive to increase behavior).
Negative punishment removes a desirable stimulus to decrease behavior — removing anxiety is not removing something desirable.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A child sees a commercial for a toy and immediately asks their parent to buy it. The fact that the child wants the toy before ever having played with it is best explained by:

choices.text: Operant conditioning — the child expects to be reinforced
choices.explanation: The child has not yet been reinforced for wanting this toy — the desire comes from watching the commercial, not from prior reinforcement.

choices.text: Observational learning — the child saw others enjoying the toy
choices.explanation: Observational learning through commercial media — seeing other children enjoying the toy — is sufficient to create desire and requests without direct experience or reinforcement.

choices.text: Classical conditioning — the toy is an unconditioned stimulus
choices.explanation: The toy has no innate reinforcing properties for this child — the desire came from watching others, not from stimulus pairing with an unconditioned stimulus.

choices.text: Latent learning — the child has hidden knowledge about the toy
choices.explanation: Latent learning involves forming cognitive maps through exploration — not being motivated by watching media depictions.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: What distinguishes conditioned taste aversion from typical classical conditioning?

choices.text: It occurs only in humans, not animals
choices.explanation: Garcia's original research was conducted with rats — taste aversion is actually easier to demonstrate in animals than in humans.

choices.text: It requires hundreds of CS-US pairings to develop
choices.explanation: Taste aversion typically develops from a single pairing — this is one of the features that distinguished Garcia's findings from standard classical conditioning assumptions.

choices.text: It can develop after a single pairing with a long delay between CS and US
choices.explanation: Unlike typical classical conditioning (which requires close temporal contiguity), taste aversion can form after one pairing with a delay of several hours — showing biological preparedness shapes what can be conditioned.

choices.text: It is an example of operant rather than classical conditioning
choices.explanation: Taste aversion is a form of classical conditioning — the taste (CS) becomes associated with illness (US/UR) to produce food avoidance (CR).

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of insight learning?

choices.text: A rat presses a lever 10 times to get food
choices.explanation: Pressing a lever for food is operant conditioning — not insight learning.

choices.text: A dog salivates when it hears a bell
choices.explanation: This is classical conditioning (Pavlov) — not insight learning.

choices.text: A chimpanzee suddenly connects two sticks to reach bananas
choices.explanation: Köhler's chimpanzees demonstrated insight learning — the sudden, 'aha' mental reorganization to solve a problem, as when Sultan the chimp connected two sticks to reach distant bananas.

choices.text: A child learns to say 'please' by watching a sibling be rewarded
choices.explanation: This is observational learning (Bandura) — the child learned by watching others, not through sudden insight.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following best describes a primary reinforcer?

choices.text: A reinforcer that has gained its reinforcing properties through association with another reinforcer
choices.explanation: This describes a secondary (conditioned) reinforcer — one that gained value through pairing with a primary reinforcer.

choices.text: A reinforcer that satisfies a biological need without prior learning
choices.explanation: Primary reinforcers (food, water, warmth, sex) are innately reinforcing because they satisfy biological survival needs — their reinforcing value does not need to be learned.

choices.text: A reinforcer that is given on a variable-ratio schedule
choices.explanation: Primary reinforcer refers to the nature of the reinforcer (biological need satisfaction), not the schedule on which it is delivered.

choices.text: The most powerful reinforcement schedule available
choices.explanation: Primary reinforcer describes the type of reinforcer (not the schedule) — and the most resistant schedule is variable-ratio, not a type of reinforcer.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research on media violence and observational learning suggests that:

choices.text: Watching violent media has no effect on behavior because the viewer knows it is not real
choices.explanation: Research (Bandura's work and subsequent studies) shows that viewing violent media can increase aggressive behavior even when viewers know it is fictional.

choices.text: Only children under age 5 are affected by violent media through observational learning
choices.explanation: Observational learning from media occurs across many age groups — Bandura's original Bobo doll studies were primarily with preschoolers, but effects have been found in older children as well.

choices.text: Observational learning from media can contribute to increased aggressive behavior
choices.explanation: Consistent with Bandura's observational learning framework, meta-analyses show a correlation between violent media exposure and increased aggressive thoughts and behavior, particularly when viewers identify with models and see aggression go unrewarded.

choices.text: Watching violent media decreases real-world violence through catharsis
choices.explanation: The catharsis hypothesis (that releasing aggressive impulses vicariously reduces real aggression) is not supported by research — observational learning studies show viewing aggression tends to increase, not decrease, aggressive behavior.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Applying learning principles to eliminate unwanted behaviors and increase desired ones is the goal of:

choices.explanation: Psychoanalysis focuses on revealing unconscious conflicts through free association and dream analysis — not on applying learning principles to change behavior.
Humanistic therapy emphasizes personal growth, self-actualization, and unconditional positive regard — not the direct application of learning principles.
Behavior therapy applies classical and operant conditioning principles to modify behavior — using techniques like systematic desensitization (classical) and token economies (operant).
Cognitive therapy focuses on changing irrational thought patterns — while CBT combines both, the specific focus on applying learning principles to change behavior is behavior therapy.
